summary: Gruber reports that CVS and Rite Aid are disabling NFC payment terminals entirely to block Apple Pay, because both retailers are part of the MCX consortium developing a competing mobile payment system called CurrentC. CurrentC uses QR codes instead of NFC, only works with debit cards and prepaid store cards, and is designed primarily to collect customer data rather than improve the payment experience. Gruber argues that retailers shutting down NFC validates Apple Pay's appeal — these terminals existed for years unused, and retailers would rather block everyone than let Apple Pay succeed. He contrasts Apple Pay's seamless Touch ID experience with CurrentC's cumbersome QR code scanning, predicting CurrentC will gain no traction. The core conflict is about data: Apple Pay doesn't share customer information with merchants, which is exactly why these retailers oppose it.
key_points: CVS and Rite Aid disabled all NFC payments to block Apple Pay, despite also killing Google Wallet in the process
CurrentC, the retailer-backed alternative, uses QR codes and only works with debit cards and store cards — not credit cards
Apple Pay is designed around customer experience while CurrentC is designed around data collection and merchant control
The fact that retailers are actively blocking NFC validates that Apple Pay is working and people are using it
Apple Pay doesn't share personal customer data with merchants, which is the real reason retailers oppose it — not security or cost
money_quotes: What Apple gets and what no one else in the industry does is that using your mobile device for payments will only work if it's far easier and better than using a credit card.

These things have been installed for years and so few people used them, apparently, that these retailers would rather block everyone than allow Apple Pay to continue working. I can't imagine a better validation of Apple Pay's appeal.

It's not about security — Apple Pay is far more secure than any credit/debit card system in the U.S. It's not about money — Apple's tiny slice of the transaction comes from the banks, not the merchants. It's about data.
They're doing this so they can pursue a system that is less secure, less convenient, and not private.
Tim Cook was exactly right on stage last month when he introduced Apple Pay: it's the only mobile payment solution designed around improving the customer experience.
QR codes. Good luck with that.
key_insight: Retailers disabling NFC to block Apple Pay is the strongest possible validation of its appeal, and their data-collection-driven alternative CurrentC sacrifices security, convenience, and privacy for merchant control.
